>Could you elaborate "if available"? What happens, if it is not available?

The build script will search the dir `MXNET_HOME/lib` first. If found a `libmxnet.so` under that dir, it will try to load it via `dlopen`. If `dlopen` failed, that file will be ignored.
In case of not available, it will build stuffs from source.

Right. I'm tempted to not define it further. I think it is suitably defined in [their spec](https://semver.org). That said, SemVer is far from perfect.

In that specific case, the way I see it is that the user was using our software to provide them with an ISO 8601 parsing function. The SemVer "interface" for ciso8601 [is defined here](https://github.com/closeio/ciso8601#error-handling). Our code failed to implement that interface, by not faithfully following the spec. So that was a bug in our system. When we release the fix, it could [be considered a patch release](https://semver.org/#spec-item-6).

As you mention, it could turn out to be the case that this type of non-conformance could be common in real world data. Someone could be relying on our library specifically because of its loose parsing, and this "patch" would cause them issues.

In that case, they could either change the upstream source of their non-conformant timestamps, or request that the `ciso8601` interface be extended to handle that case.

I'm not sure if this is the best demo of how to draw a PSF.  PSFs should have unit flux.  So it's one thing for the SED of the PSF to be flat, but it's also not normalized correctly for this drawing to make sense.  So rather than divide by the sum to get back to unit flux, I think it would be better to show how to get the flux to 1 in the first place.
```
# Approximate a point source.
point = galsim.Gaussian(sigma=1.e-8, flux = 1)
# Use a flat SED here, but could use something else.  A stellar SED for instance.  
# Or a typical galaxy SED.  Depending on your purpose for drawing the PSF.
star_sed = galsim.SED(lambda x:1).withFlux(1.,filter_)  # Give it unit flux.
star = galsim.Convolve(point * star_sed, PSF)   
star.drawImage(bandpass=filter_, image=img_psf, scale=wfirst.pixel_scale)
```

Flake8 doesn't know what to do with that rule, so it just skips it.  But it's actually the most important rule in the whole thing.  And it's why I can't stand style linters. ```python
```python
def _skellington(things, methname, key_extractor, connection=None):
    """Helper template method for :func:`put` and :func:`delete`.
    """
    if not things:
        return

    connection = connection or _implicit_environ.CONNECTION

    current = _BATCHES.top
    in_batch = current is not None

    if not in_batch:
        keys = [key_extractor(thing) for thing in things]
        dataset_id = _get_dataset_id_from_keys(keys)
        current = Batch(dataset_id=dataset_id, connection=connection)

    method = getattr(current, methname)

    for thing in things:
        method(thing)

    if not in_batch:
        current.commit()
```

For the 2D case, I've trying to get an intuitive understanding of what these coefficients/constrants should be. Here's what I've been able to dig up so far.

Video tutorial (skipping to the constant part): https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=A8tWMnr5pps&feature=youtu.be&t=11m22s

Written up derivation of Sabine: https://courses.physics.illinois.edu/phys406/sp2017/Lecture_Notes/P406POM_Lecture_Notes/Derivation_of_the_Sabine_Equation.pdf

So in the well-known [3D case](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reverberation#Sabine_equation) there's this constant factor of `24` which seems to come from a factor `4` which comes computing the surface area of the sphere multiplied by a factor `6` dropping down from `ln(10^-6)` for the `-60` drop.

So for 2D, I think the combined factor should be `2*6=12` where `2` comes from the area of a circle and the `6` still comes from the `-60 dB` drop.

The name provided by the user may be an acceptable and recognized domain, but the CA is checking that they have control over the domain by verifying they were able to configure a DNS record as requested. Here's two links for seeing how this is normally described in this context:

* https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-06#section-8
* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Domain-validated_certificate

I hope this makes sense and feel free to either update this sentence or delete it entirely. After that, I'll merge this PR!

This misses a few use cases, however unlikely they may be:

* Water may be a component of the atmosphere/ocean system, but may not be the major condensible (like Mars). Hence, I want to keep condensible separate from water.

* Astrophysics graduates who start their modelling career with say a Titan GCM may
not find 'water' to mean the condensible in their models.

* I don't want to replace Sympl's constants for the same reasons as your
second bullet point.

The way this constant library is written now, with the default set of constants, solid phase/ice and liquid phase/water are interchangeable. So, Sympl's constants can be used without issue for earth models.

But I think it is worth thinking about whether we want to address these issues at the Sympl level itself (since it is ultimately meant to model any planet). I am open to moving some of this code into Sympl.
